rows.pali: 365.Idāni yāsaṃ lokikābhiññānaṃ vasena ayaṃ samādhibhāvanā abhiññānisaṃsāti vuttā, tā abhiññā sampādetuṃ yasmā pathavīkasiṇādīsu adhigatacatutthajjhānena yoginā yogo kātabbo.
rows.english: 1. It was said above with reference to the mundane kinds of direct- knowledge that this development of concentration “provides … the benefit of the kinds of direct-knowledge” (XI.122). Now, in order to perfect those kinds of direct-knowledge the task must be undertaken by a meditator who has reached the fourth jhāna in the earth kasiṇa, and so on.

rows.pali: Evañhissa sā samādhibhāvanā adhigatānisaṃsā ceva bhavissati thiratarā ca, so adhigatānisaṃsāya thiratarāya samādhibhāvanāya samannāgato sukheneva paññābhāvanaṃ sampādessati.
rows.english: And in doing this, not only will this development of concentration have provided benefits in this way, it will also have become more advanced; and when he thus possesses concentration so developed as to have both provided benefits and become more advanced, he will then more easily perfect the development of understanding.

rows.pali: Tasmā abhiññākathaṃ tāva ārabhissāma.
rows.english: So meanwhile we shall deal with the explanation of the kinds of direct-knowledge now.

rows.pali: Bhagavatā hi adhigatacatutthajjhānasamādhīnaṃ kulaputtānaṃ samādhibhāvanānisaṃsadassanatthañceva uttaruttari paṇītapaṇītadhammadesanatthañca "so evaṃ samāhite citte parisuddhe pariyodāte anaṅgaṇe vigatūpakkilese mudubhūte kammaniye ṭhite āneñjappatte iddhividhāya cittaṃ abhinīharati abhininnāmeti.
rows.english: 2.In order to show the benefits of developing concentration to clansmen whose concentration has reached the fourth jhāna, and in order to teach progressively refined Dhamma, five kinds of mundane direct-knowledge have been described by the Blessed One. They are: (1) the kinds of supernormal power, described in the way beginning, “When his concentrated mind is thus purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilement, and has become malleable, wieldy, steady, and attained to imperturbability,1 he directs, he inclines, his mind to the kinds of supernormal power.

rows.pali: So anekavihitaṃ iddhividhaṃ paccanubhoti ekopi hutvā bahudhā hotī"tiādinā (dī. ni. 1.238) nayena iddhividhaṃ, dibbasotadhātuñāṇaṃ, cetopariyañāṇaṃ, pubbenivāsānussatiñāṇaṃ, sattānaṃ cutūpapāte ñāṇanti pañca lokikābhiññā vuttā.
rows.english: He wields the various kinds of supernormal power. Having been one, he becomes many …” (D I 77); (2) the knowledge of the divine ear element; (3) the knowledge of penetration of minds; (4) the knowledge of recollection of past lives; and (5) the knowledge of the passing away and reappearance of beings.

rows.pali: Tattha ekopi hutvā bahudhā hotītiādikaṃ iddhivikubbanaṃ kātukāmena ādikammikena yoginā odātakasiṇapariyantesu aṭṭhasu kasiṇesu aṭṭha aṭṭha samāpattiyo nibbattetvā –
rows.english: If a meditator wants to begin performing the transformation by supernormal power described as, “Having been one, he becomes many,” etc., he must achieve the eight attainments in each of the eight kasiṇas ending with the white kasiṇa. He must also have complete control of his mind in the following fourteen ways: [374]

rows.pali: Kasiṇānulomato, kasiṇapaṭilomato, kasiṇānulomapaṭilomato, jhānānulomato, jhānapaṭilomato, jhānānulomapaṭilomato, jhānukkantikato, kasiṇukkantikato, jhānakasiṇukkantikato, aṅgasaṅkantito, ārammaṇasaṅkantito, aṅgārammaṇasaṅkantito, aṅgavavatthāpanato, ārammaṇavavatthāpanatoti.
rows.english: (i) in the order of the kasiṇa, (ii) in the reverse order of the kasiṇa, (iii) in the order and reverse order of the kasiṇa, (iv) in the order of the jhāna, (v) in the reverse order of the jhāna (vi) in the order and reverse order of the jhāna, (vii) skipping jhāna, (viii) skipping kasiṇa, (ix) skipping jhāna and kasiṇa, (x) transposition of factors, (xi) transposition of object, (xii) transposition of factors and object, (xiii) definition of factors, and (xiv) definition of object.

rows.pali: Imehi cuddasahi ākārehi cittaṃ paridametabbaṃ.
rows.english: 3. But what is “in the order of the kasiṇa” here?

rows.pali: 366.Katamaṃ panettha kasiṇānulomaṃ - pe - katamaṃ ārammaṇavavatthāpananti.
rows.english: What is “definition of object”?

rows.pali: Idha bhikkhu pathavīkasiṇe jhānaṃ samāpajjati, tato āpokasiṇeti evaṃ paṭipāṭiyā aṭṭhasu kasiṇesu satakkhattumpi sahassakkhattumpi samāpajjati, idaṃ kasiṇānulomaṃ nāma.
rows.english: (i) Here a bhikkhu attains jhāna in the earth kasiṇa, after that in the water kasiṇa, and so progressing through the eight kasiṇas, doing so even a hundred times, even a thousand times, in each one. This is called in the order of the kasiṇas.

rows.pali: Odātakasiṇato pana paṭṭhāya tatheva paṭilomakkamena samāpajjanaṃ kasiṇapaṭilomaṃ nāma.
rows.english: (ii) Attaining them in like manner in reverse order, starting with the white kasiṇa, is called in the reverse order of the kasiṇas.

rows.pali: Pathavīkasiṇato paṭṭhāya yāva odātakasiṇaṃ, odātakasiṇatopi paṭṭhāya yāva pathavīkasiṇanti evaṃ anulomapaṭilomavasena punappunaṃ samāpajjanaṃ kasiṇānulomapaṭilomaṃ nāma.
rows.english: (iii) Attaining them again and again in forward and reverse order, from the earth kasiṇa up to the white kasiṇa and from the white kasiṇa back to the earth kasiṇa, is called in the order and reverse order of the kasiṇas.

rows.pali: Paṭhamajjhānato pana paṭṭhāya paṭipāṭiyā yāva nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṃ, tāva punappunaṃ samāpajjanaṃ jhānānulomaṃ nāma.
rows.english: 4.(iv) Attaining again and again from the first jhāna up to the base consisting of neither perception nor non-perception is called in the order of the jhānas.

rows.pali: Nevasaññānāsaññāyatanato paṭṭhāya yāva paṭhamajjhānaṃ, tāva punappunaṃ samāpajjanaṃ jhānapaṭilomaṃ nāma.
rows.english: (v) Attaining again and again from the base consisting of neither perception nor non-perception back to the first jhāna is called in the reverse order of the jhānas.

rows.pali: Paṭhamajjhānato paṭṭhāya yāva nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṃ, nevasaññānāsaññāyatanato paṭṭhāya yāva paṭhamajjhānanti evaṃ anulomapaṭilomavasena punappunaṃ samāpajjanaṃ jhānānulomapaṭilomaṃ nāma.
rows.english: (vi) Attaining in forward and reverse order, from the first jhāna up to the base consisting of neither perception nor non-perception and from the base consisting of neither perception nor non-perception back to the first jhāna, is called in the order and reverse order of the jhānas.

rows.pali: Pathavīkasiṇe pana paṭhamaṃ jhānaṃ samāpajjitvā tattheva tatiyaṃ samāpajjati, tato tadeva ugghāṭetvā ākāsānañcāyatanaṃ, tato ākiñcaññāyatananti evaṃ kasiṇaṃ anukkamitvā jhānasseva ekantarikabhāvena ukkamanaṃ jhānukkantikaṃ nāma.
rows.english: 5.(vii) He skips alternate jhānas without skipping the kasiṇas in the following way: having first attained the first jhāna in the earth kasiṇa, he attains the third jhāna in that same kasiṇa, and after that, having removed [the kasiṇa (X.6), he attains] the base consisting of boundless space, after that the base consisting of nothingness. This is called skipping jhānas.

rows.pali: Evaṃ āpokasiṇādimūlikāpi yojanā kātabbā.
rows.english: And that based on the water kasiṇa, etc., should be construed similarly.

rows.pali: Pathavīkasiṇe paṭhamaṃ jhānaṃ samāpajjitvā puna tadeva tejokasiṇe, tato nīlakasiṇe, tato lohitakasiṇeti iminā nayena jhānaṃ anukkamitvā kasiṇasseva ekantarikabhāvena ukkamanaṃ kasiṇukkantikaṃ nāma.
rows.english: (viii) When he skips alternate kasiṇas without skipping jhānas in the following way: having attained the first jhāna in the earth kasiṇa, he again attains that same jhāna in the fire kasiṇa and then in the blue kasiṇa and then in the red kasiṇa, this is called skipping kasiṇas.

rows.pali: Pathavīkasiṇe paṭhamaṃ jhānaṃ samāpajjitvā tato tejokasiṇe tatiyaṃ, nīlakasiṇaṃ ugghāṭetvā ākāsānañcāyatanaṃ, lohitakasiṇato ākiñcaññāyatananti iminā nayena jhānassa ceva kasiṇassa ca ukkamanaṃ jhānakasiṇukkantikaṃ nāma.
rows.english: (ix) When he skips both jhānas and kasiṇas in the following way: having attained the first jhāna in the earth kasiṇa, he next attains the third in the fire kasiṇa, next the base consisting of boundless space after removing the blue kasiṇa, next the base consisting of nothingness [arrived at] from the red kasiṇa, this is called skipping jhānas and kasiṇas.

rows.pali: Pathavīkasiṇe pana paṭhamaṃ jhānaṃ samāpajjitvā tattheva itaresampi samāpajjanaṃ aṅgasaṅkantikaṃ nāma.
rows.english: 6.(x) Attaining the first jhāna in the earth kasiṇa [375] and then attaining the others in that same kasiṇa is called transposition of factors.

rows.pali: Pathavīkasiṇe paṭhamaṃ jhānaṃ samāpajjitvā tadeva āpokasiṇe - pe - tadeva odātakasiṇeti evaṃ sabbakasiṇesu ekasseva jhānassa samāpajjanaṃ ārammaṇasaṅkantikaṃ nāma.
rows.english: (xi) Attaining the first jhāna in the earth kasiṇa and then that same jhāna in the water kasiṇa … in the white kasiṇa is called transposition of object.

rows.pali: Pathavīkasiṇe paṭhamaṃ jhānaṃ samāpajjitvā āpokasiṇe dutiyaṃ, tejokasiṇe tatiyaṃ, vāyokasiṇe catutthaṃ, nīlakasiṇaṃ ugghāṭetvā ākāsānañcāyatanaṃ, pītakasiṇato viññāṇañcāyatanaṃ, lohitakasiṇato ākiñcaññāyatanaṃ, odātakasiṇato nevasaññānāsaññāyatananti evaṃ ekantarikavasena aṅgānañca ārammaṇānañca saṅkamanaṃ aṅgārammaṇasaṅkantikaṃ nāma.
rows.english: (xii) Transposition of object and factors together takes place in the following way: he attains the first jhāna in the earth kasiṇa, the second jhāna in the water kasiṇa, the third in the fire kasiṇa, the fourth in the air kasiṇa, the base consisting of boundless space by removing the blue kasiṇa, the base consisting of boundless consciousness [arrived at] from the yellow kasiṇa, the base consisting of nothingness from the red kasiṇa, and the base consisting of neither perception nor non-perception from the white kasiṇa. This is called transposition of factors and object.

rows.pali: Paṭhamaṃ jhānaṃ pana pañcaṅgikanti vavatthapetvā dutiyaṃ tivaṅgikaṃ, tatiyaṃ duvaṅgikaṃ, tathā catutthaṃ ākāsānañcāyatanaṃ - pe - nevasaññānāsaññāyatananti evaṃ jhānaṅgamattasseva vavatthāpanaṃ aṅgavavatthāpanaṃ nāma.
rows.english: 7.(xiii) The defining of only the jhāna factors by defining the first jhāna as five- factored, the second as three-factored, the third as two-factored, and likewise the fourth, the base consisting of boundless space, … and the base consisting of neither perception nor non-perception, is called definition of factors.

rows.pali: Tathā idaṃ pathavīkasiṇanti vavatthapetvā idaṃ āpokasiṇaṃ - pe - idaṃ odātakasiṇanti evaṃ ārammaṇamattasseva vavatthāpanaṃ ārammaṇavavatthāpanaṃ nāma.
rows.english: (xiv) Likewise, the defining of only the object as “This is the earth kasiṇa,” “This is the water kasiṇa” … “This is the white kasiṇa,” is called definition of object.

rows.pali: Aṅgārammaṇavavatthāpanampi eke icchanti.
rows.english: Some would also have “defining of factors and object”;

rows.pali: Aṭṭhakathāsu pana anāgatattā addhā taṃ bhāvanāmukhaṃ na hoti.
rows.english: but since that is not given in the commentaries it is certainly not a heading in the development.

rows.pali: 367.Imehi pana cuddasahi ākārehi cittaṃ aparidametvā pubbe abhāvitabhāvano ādikammiko yogāvacaro iddhivikubbanaṃ sampādessatīti netaṃ ṭhānaṃ vijjati.
rows.english: 8. It is not possible for a meditator to begin to accomplish transformation by supernormal powers unless he has previously completed his development by controlling his mind in these fourteen ways.

rows.pali: Ādikammikassa hi kasiṇaparikammampi bhāro, satesu sahassesu vā ekova sakkoti.
rows.english: Now, the kasiṇa preliminary work is difficult for a beginner and only one in a hundred or a thousand can do it.

rows.pali: Katakasiṇaparikammassa nimittuppādanaṃ bhāro, satesu sahassesu vā ekova sakkoti.
rows.english: The arousing of the sign is difficult for one who has done the preliminary work and only one in a hundred or a thousand can do it.

rows.pali: Uppanne nimitte taṃ vaḍḍhetvā appanādhigamo bhāro, satesu sahassesu vā ekova sakkoti.
rows.english: To extend the sign when it has arisen and to reach absorption is difficult and only one in a hundred or a thousand can do it.

rows.pali: Adhigatappanassa cuddasahākārehi cittaparidamanaṃ bhāro, satesu sahassesu vā ekova sakkoti.
rows.english: To tame one’s mind in the fourteen ways after reaching absorption is difficult and only one in a hundred or a thousand can do it.

rows.pali: Cuddasahākārehi paridamitacittassāpi iddhivikubbanaṃ nāma bhāro, satesu sahassesu vā ekova sakkoti.
rows.english: The transformation by supernormal power after training one’s mind in the fourteen ways is difficult and only one in a hundred or a thousand can do it.

rows.pali: Vikubbanappattassāpi khippanisantibhāvo nāma bhāro, satesu sahassesu vā ekova khippanisantī hoti.
rows.english: Rapid response after attaining transformation is difficult and only one in a hundred or a thousand can do it.

rows.pali: Therambatthale mahārohaṇaguttattherassa gilānupaṭṭhānaṃ āgatesu tiṃsamattesu iddhimantasahassesu upasampadāya aṭṭhavassiko rakkhitatthero viya.
rows.english: 9. Like the Elder Rakkhita who, eight years after his full admission to the Order, was in the midst of thirty thousand bhikkhus possessing supernormal powers who had come to attend upon the sickness of the Elder Mahā-Rohaṇa- Gutta at Therambatthala.

rows.pali: Tassānubhāvo pathavīkasiṇaniddese (visuddhi. 1.78 ādayo) vuttoyeva.
rows.english: His feat is mentioned under the earth kasiṇa (IV.135).

rows.pali: Taṃ panassānubhāvaṃ disvā thero āha "āvuso, sace rakkhito nābhavissa sabbe garahappattā assāma 'nāgarājānaṃ rakkhituṃ nāsakkhiṃsū'ti.
rows.english: Seeing his feat, an elder said, “Friends, if Rakkhita had not been there, we should have been put to shame. [It could have been said], ‘They were unable to protect the royal nāga.’

rows.pali: Tasmā attanā gahetvā vicaritabbaṃ āvudhaṃ nāma malaṃ sodhetvāva gahetvā vicarituṃ vaṭṭatī"ti.
rows.english: So we ourselves ought to go about [with our abilities perfected], just as it is proper (for soldiers) to go about with weapons cleaned of stains.”

rows.pali: Te therassa ovāde ṭhatvā tiṃsasahassāpi bhikkhū khippanisantino ahesuṃ.
rows.english: The thirty thousand bhikkhus heeded the elder’s advice and achieved rapid response.

rows.pali: Khippanisantiyāpi ca sati parassa patiṭṭhābhāvo bhāro, satesu sahassesu vā ekova hoti, giribhaṇḍavāhanapūjāya mārena aṅgāravasse pavattite ākāse pathaviṃ māpetvā aṅgāravassaparittārako thero viya.
rows.english: 10. And helping another after acquiring rapidity in responding is difficult and only one in a hundred or a thousand can do it. Like the elder who gave protection against the rain of embers by creating earth in the sky, when the rain of embers was produced by Māra at the Giribhaṇḍavahana offering. 2

rows.pali: Balavapubbayogānaṃ pana buddhapaccekabuddhaaggasāvakādīnaṃ vināpi iminā vuttappakārena bhāvanānukkamena arahattapaṭilābheneva idañca iddhivikubbanaṃ aññe ca paṭisambhidādibhedā guṇā ijjhanti.
rows.english: 11. It is only in Buddhas, Paccekabuddhas, chief disciples, etc., who have vast previous endeavour behind them, that this transformation by supernormal power and other such special qualities as the discriminations are brought to success simply with the attainment of Arahantship and without the progressive course of development of the kind just described.

rows.pali: Tasmā yathā piḷandhanavikatiṃ kattukāmo suvaṇṇakāro aggidhamanādīhi suvaṇṇaṃ muduṃ kammaññaṃ katvāva karoti, yathā ca bhājanavikatiṃ kattukāmo kumbhakāro mattikaṃ suparimadditaṃ muduṃ katvā karoti, evameva ādikammikena imehi cuddasahākārehi cittaṃ paridametvā chandasīsacittasīsavīriyasīsavīmaṃsāsīsasamāpajjanavasena ceva āvajjanādivasībhāvavasena ca muduṃ kammaññaṃ katvā iddhividhāya yogo karaṇīyo.
rows.english: 12. So just as when a goldsmith wants to make some kind of ornament, he does so only after making the gold malleable and wieldy by smelting it, etc., and just as when a potter wants to make some kind of vessel, he does so only after making the clay well kneaded and malleable, a beginner too must likewise prepare for the kinds of supernormal powers by controlling his mind in these fourteen ways; and he must do so also by making his mind malleable and wieldy both by attaining under the headings of zeal, consciousness, energy, and inquiry,3 and by mastery in adverting, and so on.

rows.pali: Pubbahetusampannena pana kasiṇesu catutthajjhānamatte ciṇṇavasināpi kātuṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: But one who already has the required condition for it owing to practice in previous lives needs only prepare himself by acquiring mastery in the fourth jhāna in the kasiṇas.

rows.pali: Yathā panettha yogo kātabbo, taṃ vidhiṃ dassento bhagavā "so evaṃ samāhite citte"tiādimāha.
rows.english: 13. Now, the Blessed One showed how the preparation should be done in saying, “When his concentrated mind,” and so on.

rows.pali: 368.Tatrāyaṃ pāḷinayānusāreneva vinicchayakathā.
rows.english: Here is the explanation, which follows the text (see §2).

rows.pali: Tattha soti so adhigatacatutthajjhāno yogī.
rows.english: Herein, he is a meditator who has attained the fourth jhāna.

rows.pali: Evanti catutthajjhānakkamanidassanametaṃ.
rows.english: Thus signifies the order in which the fourth jhāna comes;

rows.pali: Iminā paṭhamajjhānādhigamādinā kamena catutthajjhānaṃ paṭilabhitvāti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: having obtained the fourth jhāna in this order beginning with attaining the first jhāna, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Samāhiteti iminā catutthajjhānasamādhinā samāhite.
rows.english: Concentrated: concentrated by means of the fourth jhāna.

rows.pali: Citteti rūpāvacaracitte.
rows.english: Mind: fine-material-sphere consciousness.

rows.pali: Parisuddhetiādīsu pana upekkhāsatipārisuddhibhāvena parisuddhe.
rows.english: 14. But as to the words “purified,” etc., it is purified by means of the state of mindfulness purified by equanimity.

rows.pali: Parisuddhattāyeva pariyodāte, pabhassareti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: It is bright precisely because it is purified; it is limpid (see A I 10), is what is meant.

rows.pali: Sukhādīnaṃ paccayānaṃ ghātena vihatarāgādiaṅgaṇattā anaṅgaṇe.
rows.english: It is unblemished since the blemishes consisting of greed, etc., are eliminated by the removal of their conditions consisting of bliss, and the rest.

rows.pali: Anaṅgaṇattāyeva vigatūpakkilese.
rows.english: It is rid of defilement precisely because it is unblemished;

rows.pali: Aṅgaṇena hi taṃ cittaṃ upakkilissati.
rows.english: for it is by the blemish that the consciousness becomes defiled.

rows.pali: Subhāvitattā mudubhūte, vasībhāvappatteti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: It has become malleable because it is well developed; it suffers mastery, is what is meant,

rows.pali: Vase vattamānaṃ hi cittaṃ mudunti vuccati.
rows.english: for consciousness that suffers mastery is called “malleable.”

rows.pali: Muduttāyeva ca kammaniye, kammakkhame kammayoggeti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: It is wieldy (kammanīya) precisely because it is malleable; it suffers being worked (kammakkhama), is fit to be worked (kammayogga), is what is meant.

rows.pali: Muduṃ hi cittaṃ kammaniyaṃ hoti sudantamiva suvaṇṇaṃ, tañca ubhayampi subhāvitattāyevāti.
rows.english: 15. For a malleable consciousness is wieldy, like well-smelted gold; and it is both of these because it is well developed,

rows.pali: Yathāha "nāhaṃ, bhikkhave, aññaṃ ekadhammampi samanupassāmi, yaṃ evaṃ bhāvitaṃ bahulīkataṃ muduñca hoti kammaniyañca, yathayidaṃ, bhikkhave, citta"nti (a. ni. 1.22).
rows.english: according as it is said: “Bhikkhus, I do not see anyone thing that, when developed and cultivated, becomes so malleable and wieldy as does the mind” (A I 9).

rows.pali: Etesu parisuddhabhāvādīsu ṭhitattā ṭhite.
rows.english: 16. It is steady because it is steadied in this purifiedness, and the rest.

rows.pali: Ṭhitattāyeva āneñjappatte, acale niriñjaneti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: It is attained to imperturbability (āneñjappatta) precisely because it is steady; it is motionless, without perturbation (niriñjana), is what is meant.

rows.pali: Mudukammaññabhāvena vā attano vase ṭhitattā ṭhite.
rows.english: Or alternatively, it is steady because steady in its own masterability through malleability and wieldiness,

rows.pali: Saddhādīhi pariggahitattā āneñjappatte.
rows.english: and it is attained to imperturbability because it is reinforced by faith, and so on.

rows.pali: Saddhāpariggahitaṃ hi cittaṃ assaddhiyena na iñjati.
rows.english: 17. For consciousness reinforced by faith is not perturbed by faithlessness;

rows.pali: Vīriyapariggahitaṃ kosajjena na iñjati.
rows.english: when reinforced by energy, it is not perturbed by idleness;

rows.pali: Satipariggahitaṃ pamādena na iñjati.
rows.english: when reinforced by mindfulness, it is not perturbed by negligence;

rows.pali: Samādhipariggahitaṃ uddhaccena na iñjati.
rows.english: when reinforced by concentration, it is not perturbed by agitation;

rows.pali: Paññāpariggahitaṃ avijjāya na iñjati.
rows.english: when reinforced by understanding, it is not perturbed by ignorance;

rows.pali: Obhāsagataṃ kilesandhakārena na iñjati.
rows.english: and when illuminated, it is not perturbed by the darkness of defilement.

rows.pali: Imehi chahi dhammehi pariggahitaṃ āneñjappattaṃ hoti.
rows.english: So when it is reinforced by these six states, it is attained to imperturbability.

rows.pali: Evaṃ aṭṭhaṅgasamannāgataṃ cittaṃ abhinīhārakkhamaṃ hoti abhiññāsacchikaraṇīyānaṃ dhammānaṃ abhiññāsacchikiriyāya.
rows.english: 18. Consciousness possessing these eight factors in this way is susceptible of being directed to the realization by direct-knowledge of states realizable by direct-knowledge.

rows.pali: Aparo nayo, catutthajjhānasamādhinā samāhite.
rows.english: 19. Another method: It is concentrated by means of fourth-jhāna concentration.

rows.pali: Nīvaraṇadūrabhāvena parisuddhe.
rows.english: It is purified by separation from the hindrances.

rows.pali: Vitakkādisamatikkamena pariyodāte.
rows.english: It is bright owing to the surmounting of applied thought and the rest.

rows.pali: Jhānapaṭilābhapaccayānaṃ icchāvacarānaṃ abhāvena anaṅgaṇe.
rows.english: It is unblemished owing to absence of evil wishes based on the obtainment of jhāna.4

rows.pali: Abhijjhādīnaṃ cittassa upakkilesānaṃ vigamena vigatūpakkilese.
rows.english: It is rid of defilement owing to the disappearance of the defilements of the mind consisting in covetousness, etc.;

rows.pali: Ubhayampi cetaṃ anaṅgaṇasuttavatthasuttānusārena (ma. ni. 1.57 ādayo) veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: and both of these should be understood according to the Anaṅgaṇa Sutta (MN 5) and the Vattha Sutta (MN 7).

rows.pali: Vasippattiyā mudubhūte.
rows.english: It is become malleable by masterability.

rows.pali: Iddhipādabhāvūpagamena kammaniye.
rows.english: It is wieldy by reaching the state of a road to power (§50).

rows.pali: Bhāvanāpāripūriyā paṇītabhāvūpagamena ṭhite āneñjappatte.
rows.english: It is steady and attained to imperturbability by reaching the refinement of completed development;

rows.pali: Yathā āneñjappattaṃ hoti, evaṃ ṭhiteti attho.
rows.english: the meaning is that according as it has attained imperturbability so it is steady.

rows.pali: Evampi aṭṭhaṅgasamannāgataṃ cittaṃ abhinīhārakkhamaṃ hoti abhiññāsacchikaraṇīyānaṃ dhammānaṃ abhiññāsacchikiriyāya pādakaṃ padaṭṭhānabhūtanti.
rows.english: And the consciousness possessing these eight factors in this way is susceptible of being directed to the realization by direct-knowledge of states realizable by direct-knowledge, since it is the basis, the proximate cause, for them.

rows.pali: 369.Iddhividhāya cittaṃ abhinīharati abhininnāmetīti ettha ijjhanaṭṭhena iddhi, nipphattiatthena paṭilābhaṭṭhena cāti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 20. He directs, he inclines, his mind to the kinds of supernormal powers (iddhi- vidha—lit. “kinds of success”): here “success” (iddhi) is the success of succeeding (ijjhana); in the sense of production, in the sense of obtainment, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Yañhi nipphajjati paṭilabbhati ca, taṃ ijjhatīti vuccati.
rows.english: For what is produced and obtained is called “successful,”

rows.pali: Yathāha "kāmaṃ kāmayamānassa, tassa cetaṃ samijjhatī"ti (su. ni. 772).
rows.english: according as it is said, “When a mortal desires, if his desire is fulfilled” (samijjhati) (Sn 766),

rows.pali: Tathā "nekkhammaṃ ijjhatīti iddhi, paṭiharatīti pāṭihāriyaṃ.
rows.english: and likewise: “Renunciation succeeds (ijjhati), thus it is a success (iddhi) … It metamorphoses (paṭiharati) [lust], thus it is a metamorphosis (pāṭihāriya)5 …

rows.pali: Arahattamaggo ijjhatīti iddhi, paṭiharatīti pāṭihāriya"nti (paṭi. ma. 3.32).
rows.english: The Arahant path succeeds, thus it is a success … It metamorphoses [all defilements], thus it is a metamorphosis” (Paṭis II 229).

rows.pali: Aparo nayo, ijjhanaṭṭhena iddhi.
rows.english: 21. Another method: success is in the sense of succeeding.

rows.pali: Upāyasampadāyetamadhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: That is a term for the effectiveness of the means;

rows.pali: Upāyasampadā hi ijjhati adhippetaphalappasavanato.
rows.english: for effectiveness of the means succeeds with the production of the result intended,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "ayaṃ kho citto gahapati sīlavā kalyāṇadhammo, sace paṇidahissati 'anāgatamaddhānaṃ rājā assaṃ cakkavattī'ti, tassa kho ayaṃ ijjhissati sīlavato cetopaṇidhi visuddhattā"ti (saṃ. ni. 4.352).
rows.english: according as it is said: “This householder Citta is virtuous and magnanimous. If he should aspire, ‘Let me in the future become a Wheel-turning Monarch,’ being virtuous, he will succeed in his aspiration, because it is purified” (S IV 303).

rows.pali: Aparo nayo, etāya sattā ijjhantīti iddhi.
rows.english: 22. Another method: beings succeed by its means, thus it is success.

rows.pali: Ijjhantīti iddhā vuddhā ukkaṃsagatā hontīti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: They succeed, thus they are successful; they are enriched, promoted, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Sā dasavidhā.
Yathāha "kati iddhiyoti dasa iddhiyo".
Puna caparaṃ āha "katamā dasa iddhiyo ?

Adhiṭṭhānā iddhi, vikubbanā iddhi, manomayā iddhi, ñāṇavipphārā iddhi, samādhivipphārā iddhi, ariyā iddhi, kammavipākajā iddhi, puññavato iddhi, vijjāmayā iddhi, tattha tattha sammāpayogapaccayā ijjhanaṭṭhena iddhī"ti (paṭi. ma. 3.9).
370.Tattha "pakatiyā eko bahukaṃ āvajjati.
rows.english: 23. (i) Herein, “Normally one, he adverts to [himself as] many

rows.pali: Sataṃ vā sahassaṃ vā satasahassaṃ vā āvajjitvā ñāṇena adhiṭṭhāti 'bahuko homī"'ti (paṭi. ma. 3.10) evaṃ vibhajitvā dassitā iddhi adhiṭṭhānavasena nipphannattā adhiṭṭhānā iddhi nāma.
rows.english: or a hundred or a thousand or a hundred thousand; having adverted, he resolves with knowledge, “Let me be many” (Paṭis II 207), is called success by resolve because it is produced by resolving. the success shown in the exposition [of the above summary] thus (above).

rows.pali: 371."So pakativaṇṇaṃ vijahitvā kumārakavaṇṇaṃ vā dasseti nāgavaṇṇaṃ vā - pe - vividhampi senābyūhaṃ dassetī"ti (paṭi. ma. 3.13) evaṃ āgatā iddhi pakativaṇṇavijahanavikāravasena pavattattā vikubbanā iddhi nāma.
rows.english: 24. (ii) That given as follows, “Having abandoned his normal form, he shows [himself in] the form of a boy or the form of a serpent … or he shows a manifold military array” (Paṭis II 210), is called success as transformation because of the abandoning and alteration of the normal form.

rows.pali: 372."Idha bhikkhu imamhā kāyā aññaṃ kāyaṃ abhinimmināti rūpiṃ manomaya"nti (paṭi. ma. 3.14) iminā nayena āgatā iddhi sarīrabbhantare aññasseva manomayassa sarīrassa nipphattivasena pavattattā manomayā iddhi nāma.
rows.english: 25. (iii) That given in this way, “Here a bhikkhu creates out of this body another body possessing visible form, mind-made” (Paṭis II 210), is called success as the mind-made (body) because it occurs as the production of another, mind-made, body inside the body.

rows.pali: 373.Ñāṇuppattito pana pubbe vā pacchā vā taṃkhaṇe vā ñāṇānubhāvanibbatto viseso ñāṇavipphārā iddhi nāma.
rows.english: 26. (iv) A distinction brought about by the influence of knowledge either before the arising of the knowledge or after it or at that moment is called success by intervention of knowledge;

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ – "aniccānupassanāya niccasaññāya pahānaṭṭho ijjhatīti ñāṇavipphārā iddhi - pe - arahattamaggena sabbakilesānaṃ pahānaṭṭho ijjhatīti ñāṇavipphārā iddhi.
rows.english: for this is said: “The meaning (purpose) as abandoning perception of permanence succeeds through contemplation of impermanence, thus it is success by intervention of knowledge … The meaning (purpose) as abandoning all defilements succeeds through the Arahant path, thus it is success by intervention of knowledge.

rows.pali: Āyasmato bākkulassa ñāṇavipphārā iddhi.
rows.english: There was success by intervention of knowledge in the venerable Bakkula.

rows.pali: Āyasmato saṃkiccassa ñāṇavipphārā iddhi.
rows.english: There was success by intervention of knowledge in the venerable Saṅkicca.

rows.pali: Āyasmato bhūtapālassa ñāṇavipphārā iddhī"ti (paṭi. ma. 3.15).
rows.english: There was success by intervention of knowledge in the venerable Bhūtapāla” (Paṭis II 211).

rows.pali: Tattha āyasmā bākkulo daharova maṅgaladivase nadiyā nhāpiyamāno dhātiyā pamādena sote patito.
rows.english: 27. Herein, when the venerable Bakkula as an infant was being bathed in the river on an auspicious day, he fell into the stream through the negligence of his nurse.

rows.pali: Tamenaṃ maccho gilitvā bārāṇasītitthaṃ agamāsi.
rows.english: A fish swallowed him and eventually came to the bathing place at Benares.

rows.pali: Tatra taṃ macchabandho gahetvā seṭṭhibhariyāya vikkiṇi.
rows.english: There it was caught by a fisherman and sold to a rich man’s wife.

rows.pali: Sā macche sinehaṃ uppādetvā ahameva naṃ pacissāmīti phālentī macchakucchiyaṃ suvaṇṇabimbaṃ viya dārakaṃ disvā putto me laddhoti somanassajātā ahosi.
rows.english: The fish interested her, and thinking to cook it herself, she slit it open.

rows.pali: Iti macchakucchiyaṃ arogabhāvo āyasmato bākkulassa pacchimabhavikassa tena attabhāvena paṭilabhitabbaarahattamaggañāṇānubhāvena nibbattattā ñāṇavipphārā iddhi nāma.
rows.english: When she did so, she saw the child like a golden image in the fish’s stomach. She was overjoyed, thinking, “At last I have got a son.” So the venerable Bakkula’s safe survival in a fish’s stomach in his last existence is called “success by intervention of knowledge” because it was brought about by the influence of the Arahant-path knowledge due to be obtained by [him in] that life.

rows.pali: Vatthu pana vitthārena kathetabbaṃ.
rows.english: But the story should be told in detail (see M-a IV 190).

rows.pali: Saṃkiccattherassa pana gabbhagatasseva mātā kālamakāsi.
rows.english: 28. The Elder Saṅkicca’s mother died while he was still in her womb.

rows.pali: Tassā citakaṃ āropetvā sūlehi vijjhitvā jhāpiyamānāya dārako sūlakoṭiyā akkhikūṭe pahāraṃ labhitvā saddaṃ akāsi.
rows.english: At the time of her cremation she was pierced by stakes and placed on a pyre. The infant received a wound on the corner of his eye from the point of a stake and made a sound.

rows.pali: Tato dārako jīvatīti otāretvā kucchiṃ phāletvā dārakaṃ ayyikāya adaṃsu.
rows.english: Then, thinking that the child must be alive, they took down the body and opened its belly. They gave the child to the grandmother.

rows.pali: So tāya paṭijaggito vuddhimanvāya pabbajitvā saha paṭisambhidāhi arahattaṃ pāpuṇi.
rows.english: Under her care he grew up, and eventually he went forth and reached Arahantship together with the discriminations.

rows.pali: Iti vuttanayeneva dārucitakāya arogabhāvo āyasmato saṃkiccassa ñāṇavipphārā iddhi nāma.
rows.english: So the venerable Saṅkicca’s safe survival on the pyre is called, “success by intervention of knowledge” in the way just stated (see Dhp-a II 240).

rows.pali: Bhūtapāladārakassa pana pitā rājagahe daliddamanusso.
rows.english: 29. The boy Bhūtapāla’s father was a poor man in Rājagaha.

rows.pali: So dārūnaṃ atthāya sakaṭena aṭaviṃ gantvā dārubhāraṃ katvā sāyaṃ nagaradvārasamīpaṃ patto.
rows.english: He went into the forest with a cart to get a load of wood. It was evening when he returned to the city gate.

rows.pali: Athassa goṇā yugaṃ ossajjitvā nagaraṃ pavisiṃsu.
rows.english: Then his oxen slipped the yoke and escaped into the city.

rows.pali: So sakaṭamūle puttakaṃ nisīdāpetvā goṇānaṃ anupadaṃ gacchanto nagarameva pāvisi.
rows.english: He seated the child beside the cart and went into the city after the oxen.

rows.pali: Tassa anikkhantasseva dvāraṃ pihitaṃ.
rows.english: Before he could come out again, the gate was closed.

rows.pali: Dārakassa vāḷayakkhānucaritepi bahinagare tiyāmarattiṃ arogabhāvo vuttanayeneva ñāṇavipphārā iddhi nāma.
rows.english: The child’s safe survival through the three watches of the night outside the city in a place infested by wild beasts and spirits is called, “success by intervention of knowledge” in the way just stated.

rows.pali: Vatthu pana vitthāretabbaṃ.
rows.english: But the story should be told in detail.

rows.pali: 374.Samādhito pubbe vā pacchā vā taṃkhaṇe vā samathānubhāvanibbatto viseso samādhivipphārā iddhi.
rows.english: 30. (v) A distinction brought about by the influence of serenity either before the concentration or after it or at that moment is called success by intervention of concentration

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ "paṭhamajjhānena nīvaraṇānaṃ pahānaṭṭho ijjhatīti samādhivipphārā iddhi - pe - nevasaññānāsaññāyatanasamāpattiyā ākiñcaññāyatanasaññāya pahānaṭṭho ijjhatīti samādhivipphārā iddhi.
rows.english: for this is said: “The meaning (purpose) as abandoning the hindrances succeeds by means of the first jhāna, thus it is success by intervention of concentration … The meaning (purpose) as abandoning the base consisting of nothingness succeeds by means of the attainment of the base consisting of neither perception nor non-perception, thus it is success by intervention of concentration.

rows.pali: Āyasmato sāriputtassa samādhivipphārā iddhi, āyasmato sañjīvassa, āyasmato khāṇukoṇḍaññassa, uttarāya upāsikāya, sāmāvatiyā upāsikāya samādhivipphārā iddhī"ti (paṭi. ma. 3.16).
rows.english: There was success by intervention of concentration in the venerable Sāriputta … in the venerable Sañjīva … in the venerable Khāṇu-Kondañña … in the laywoman devotee Uttarā … in the lay-woman devotee Sāmāvatī” (Paṭis II 211–12).

rows.pali: Tattha yadā āyasmato sāriputtassa mahāmoggallānattherena saddhiṃ kapotakandarāyaṃ viharato juṇhāya rattiyā navoropitehi kesehi ajjhokāse nisinnassa eko duṭṭhayakkho sahāyakena yakkhena vāriyamānopi sīse pahāramadāsi.
rows.english: 31. Herein, while the venerable Sāriputta was living with the Elder Mahā Moggallāna at Kapotakandarā he was sitting in the open on a moonlit night with his hair newly cut. Then a wicked spirit, though warned by his companion, gave him a blow on the head,

rows.pali: Yassa meghassa viya gajjato saddo ahosi.
rows.english: the noise of which was like a thunder clap.

rows.pali: Tadā thero tassa paharaṇasamaye samāpattiṃ appesi.
rows.english: At the time the blow was given the elder was absorbed in an attainment;

rows.pali: Athassa tena pahārena na koci ābādho ahosi.
rows.english: consequently he suffered no harm from the blow.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ tassāyasmato samādhivipphārā iddhi.
rows.english: This was success by intervention of concentration in that venerable one.

rows.pali: Vatthu pana udāne (udā. 34) āgatameva.
rows.english: The story is given in the Udāna too (Ud 39).

rows.pali: Sañjīvattheraṃ pana nirodhasamāpannaṃ kālakatoti sallakkhetvā gopālakādayo tiṇakaṭṭhagomayāni saṅkaḍḍhetvā aggiṃ adaṃsu.
rows.english: 32. While the Elder Sañjīva was in the attainment of cessation, cowherds, etc., who noticed him thought he was dead. They brought grass and sticks and cow- dung and set fire to them.

rows.pali: Therassa cīvare aṃsumattampi najjhāyittha.
rows.english: Not even a corner of the elder’s robe was burnt.

rows.pali: Ayamassa anupubbasamāpattivasena pavattasamathānubhāvanibbattattā samādhivipphārā iddhi.
rows.english: This was success by intervention of concentration in him because it was brought about by the influence of the serenity occurring in his successive attainment [of each of the eight jhānas preceding cessation].

rows.pali: Vatthu pana sutte (ma. ni. 1.507) āgatameva.
rows.english: But the story is given in the Suttas too (M I 333).

rows.pali: Khāṇukoṇḍaññatthero pana pakatiyāva samāpattibahulo.
rows.english: 33. The Elder Khāṇu Kondañña was naturally gifted in attainments.

rows.pali: So aññatarasmiṃ araññe rattiṃ samāpattiṃ appetvā nisīdi.
rows.english: He was sitting absorbed in attainment one night in a certain forest.

rows.pali: Pañcasatā corā bhaṇḍakaṃ thenetvā gacchantā "idāni amhākaṃ anupathaṃ āgacchantā natthī"ti vissamitukāmā bhaṇḍakaṃ oropayamānā "khāṇuko aya"nti maññamānā therasseva upari sabbabhaṇḍakāni ṭhapesuṃ.
rows.english: Five hundred robbers came by with stolen booty. Thinking that no one was following them and needing rest, they put the booty down. Believing the elder was a tree stump (khāṇuka), they piled all the booty on him.

rows.pali: Tesaṃ vissamitvā gacchantānaṃ paṭhamaṃ ṭhapitabhaṇḍakassa gahaṇakāle kālaparicchedavasena thero vuṭṭhāsi.
rows.english: The elder emerged at the predetermined time just as they were about to depart after resting, at the very time in fact when the one who had put his booty down first was picking it up.

rows.pali: Te therassa calanākāraṃ disvā bhītā viraviṃsu.
rows.english: When they saw the elder move, they cried out in fear.

rows.pali: Thero "mā bhāyittha upāsakā, bhikkhu aha"nti āha.
rows.english: The elder said, “Do not be afraid, lay followers; I am a bhikkhu.”

rows.pali: Te āgantvā vanditvā theragatena pasādena pabbajitvā saha paṭisambhidāhi arahattaṃ pāpuṇiṃsu (dha. pa. aṭṭha. 1.1).
rows.english: They came and paid homage. Such was their confidence in the elder that they went forth into homelessness, and they eventually reached Arahantship together with the discriminations.

rows.pali: Ayamettha pañcahi bhaṇḍakasatehi ajjhotthaṭassa therassa ābādhābhāvo samādhivipphārā iddhi.
rows.english: The absence here of harm to the elder, covered as he was by five hundred bundles of goods, was success by intervention of concentration (see Dhp-a II 254).

rows.pali: Uttarā pana upāsikā puṇṇaseṭṭhissa dhītā.
rows.english: 34. The laywoman devotee Uttarā was the daughter of a rich man called Puṇṇaka.

rows.pali: Tassā sirimā nāma gaṇikā issāpakatā tattatelakaṭāhaṃ sīse āsiñci.
rows.english: A harlot called Sirimā who was envious of her, poured a basin of hot oil over her head.

rows.pali: Uttarā taṃkhaṇaññeva mettaṃ samāpajji.
rows.english: At that moment Uttarā had attained [jhāna in], loving-kindness.

rows.pali: Telaṃ pokkharapattato udakabindu viya vivaṭṭamānaṃ agamāsi.
rows.english: The oil ran off her like water on a lotus leaf.

rows.pali: Ayamassā samādhivipphārā iddhi.
rows.english: This was success by intervention of concentration in her.

rows.pali: Vatthu pana vitthāretabbaṃ.
rows.english: But the story should be given in detail (see Dhp-a III 310; A- a I 451).

rows.pali: Sāmāvatī nāma udenassa rañño aggamahesī.
rows.english: 35. King Udena’s chief queen was called Sāmāvatī.

rows.pali: Māgaṇḍiyabrāhmaṇo attano dhītāya aggamahesiṭṭhānaṃ patthayamāno tassā vīṇāya āsīvisaṃ pakkhipāpetvā rājānaṃ āha "mahārāja, sāmāvatī taṃ māretukāmā vīṇāya āsīvisaṃ gahetvā pariharatī"ti.
rows.english: The brahman Māgaṇḍiya, who aspired to elevate his own daughter to the position of chief queen, put a poisonous snake into Sāmāvatī’s lute. Then he told the king, “Sāmāvatī wants to kill you, sire. She is carrying a poisonous snake about in her lute.”

rows.pali: Rājā taṃ disvā kupito sāmāvatiṃ vadhissāmīti dhanuṃ āropetvā visapītaṃ khurappaṃ sannayhi.
rows.english: When the king found it, he was furious. Intending to kill her, he took his bow and aimed a poisoned arrow.

rows.pali: Sāmāvatī saparivārā rājānaṃ mettāya phari.
rows.english: Sāmāvatī with her retinue pervaded the king with loving- kindness.

rows.pali: Rājā neva saraṃ khipituṃ na oropetuṃ sakkonto vedhamāno aṭṭhāsi.
rows.english: The king stood trembling, unable either to shoot the arrow or to put it away.

rows.pali: Tato naṃ devī āha "kiṃ, mahārāja, kilamasī"ti?
rows.english: Then the queen said to him, “What is it, sire, are you tired?

rows.pali: "Āma kilamāmī"ti.
rows.english: ”—“Yes, I am tired.”

rows.pali: "Tena hi dhanuṃ oropehī"ti.
rows.english: —“Then put down the bow.”

rows.pali: Saro rañño pādamūleyeva pati.
rows.english: The arrow fell at the king’s feet.

rows.pali: Tato naṃ devī "mahārāja, appaduṭṭhassa nappadussitabba"nti ovadi.
rows.english: Then the queen advised him, “Sire, one should not hate one who has no hate.”

rows.pali: Iti rañño saraṃ muñcituṃ avisahanabhāvo sāmāvatiyā upāsikāya samādhivipphārā iddhīti.
rows.english: So the king’s not daring to release the arrow was success by intervention of concentration in the laywoman Sāmāvatī (see Dhp-a I 216; A-a I 443).

rows.pali: 375.Paṭikkūlādīsu appaṭikkūlasaññivihārādikā pana ariyā iddhi nāma.
rows.english: 36. (vi) That which consists in dwelling perceiving the unrepulsive in the repulsive, etc., is called Noble Ones’ success,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "katamā ariyā iddhi?
rows.english: according as it is said: “What is Noble Ones’ success?

rows.pali: Idha – bhikkhu sace ākaṅkhati 'paṭikkūle appaṭikkūlasaññī vihareyya'nti, appaṭikkūlasaññī tattha viharati - pe - upekkhako tattha viharati sato sampajāno"ti (paṭi. ma. 3.17).
rows.english: Here, if a bhikkhu should wish, “May I dwell perceiving the unrepulsive in the repulsive,” he dwells perceiving the unrepulsive in that … he dwells in equanimity towards that, mindful and fully aware” (Paṭis II 212).

rows.pali: Ayañhi cetovasippattānaṃ ariyānaṃyeva sambhavato ariyā iddhīti vuccati.
rows.english: This is called “Noble Ones’ success” because it is only produced in Noble Ones who have reached mind mastery.

rows.pali: Etāya hi samannāgato khīṇāsavo bhikkhu paṭikkūle aniṭṭhe vatthusmiṃ mettāpharaṇaṃ vā dhātumanasikāraṃ vā karonto appaṭikkūlasaññī viharati.
rows.english: 37. For if a bhikkhu with cankers destroyed possesses this kind of success, then when in the case of a disagreeable object he is practicing pervasion with loving-kindness or giving attention to it as elements, he dwells perceiving the unrepulsive;

rows.pali: Appaṭikkūle iṭṭhe vatthusmiṃ asubhapharaṇaṃ vā aniccanti manasikāraṃ vā karonto paṭikkūlasaññī viharati.
rows.english: or when in the case of an agreeable object he is practicing pervasion with foulness or giving attention to it as impermanent, he dwells perceiving the repulsive.

rows.pali: Tathā paṭikkūlāpaṭikkūlesu tadeva mettāpharaṇaṃ vā dhātumanasikāraṃ vā karonto appaṭikkūlasaññī viharati.
rows.english: Likewise, when in the case of the repulsive and unrepulsive he is practicing that same pervasion with loving-kindness or giving attention to it as elements, he dwells perceiving the unrepulsive;

rows.pali: Appaṭikkūlapaṭikkūlesu ca tadeva asubhapharaṇaṃ vā aniccanti manasikāraṃ vā karonto paṭikkūlasaññī viharati.
rows.english: and when in the case of the unrepulsive and repulsive he is practicing that same pervasion with foulness or giving attention to it as impermanent, he dwells perceiving the repulsive.

rows.pali: Cakkhunā rūpaṃ disvā neva sumano hotītiādinā nayena vuttaṃ pana chaḷaṅgupekkhaṃ pavattayamāno paṭikkūle ca appaṭikkūle ca tadubhayaṃ abhinivajjitvā upekkhako viharati sato sampajāno.
rows.english: But when he is exercising the six-factored equanimity in the following way, “On seeing a visible object with the eye, he is neither glad nor …” (Paṭis II 213), etc., then rejecting both the repulsive and the unrepulsive, he dwells in equanimity, mindful and fully aware.

rows.pali: Paṭisambhidāyañhi "kathaṃ paṭikkūle appaṭikkūlasaññī viharati?
rows.english: 38. For the meaning of this is expounded in the Paṭisambhidā in the way beginning: “How does he dwell perceiving the unrepulsive in the repulsive?

rows.pali: Aniṭṭhasmiṃ vatthusmiṃ mettāya vā pharati dhātuso vā upasaṃharatī"tiādinā (paṭi. ma. 3.17) nayena ayameva attho vibhatto.
rows.english: In the case of a disagreeable object he pervades it with loving-kindness or he treats it as elements” (Paṭis II 212).

rows.pali: Ayaṃ cetovasippattānaṃ ariyānaṃyeva sambhavato ariyā iddhīti vuccati.
rows.english: Thus it is called, “Noble Ones’ success” because it is only produced in Noble Ones who have reached mind mastery.

rows.pali: 376.Pakkhīādīnaṃ pana vehāsagamanādikā kammavipākajā iddhi nāma.
rows.english: 39. (vii) That consisting in travelling through the air in the case of winged birds, etc., is called success born of kamma result,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "katamā kammavipākajā iddhi?
rows.english: according as it is said: “What is success born of kamma result?

rows.pali: Sabbesaṃ pakkhīnaṃ sabbesaṃ devānaṃ ekaccānaṃ manussānaṃ ekaccānañca vinipātikānaṃ ayaṃ kammavipākajā iddhī"ti (paṭi. ma. 3.18).
rows.english: That in all winged birds, in all deities, in some human beings, in some inhabitants of states of loss, is success born of kamma result” (Paṭis II 213).

rows.pali: Ettha hi sabbesaṃ pakkhīnaṃ jhānaṃ vā vipassanaṃ vā vināyeva ākāsena gamanaṃ.
rows.english: For here it is the capacity in all winged birds to travel through the air without jhāna or insight that is success born of kamma result;

rows.pali: Tathā sabbesaṃ devānaṃ paṭhamakappikānañca ekaccānaṃ manussānaṃ.
rows.english: and likewise that in all deities, and some human beings, at the beginning of the aeon,

rows.pali: Tathā piyaṅkaramātā (saṃ. ni. 1.240) yakkhinī uttaramātā phussamittā dhammaguttāti evamādīnaṃ ekaccānaṃ vinipātikānaṃ ākāsena gamanaṃ kammavipākajā iddhīti.
rows.english: and likewise that in some inhabitants of states of loss such as the female spirit Piyaṅkara’s mother (see S-a II 509), Uttara’s mother (Pv 140), Phussamittā, Dhammaguttā, and so on.

rows.pali: 377.Cakkavattiādīnaṃ vehāsagamanādikā pana puññavato iddhi nāma.
rows.english: 40. (viii) That consisting in travelling through the air, etc., in the case of Wheel- turning Monarchs, etc., is called success of the meritorious,

rows.pali: Yathāha "katamā puññavato iddhi?
rows.english: according as it is said: “What is success of the meritorious?

rows.pali: Rājā cakkavattī vehāsaṃ gacchati saddhiṃ caturaṅginiyā senāya antamaso assabandhagobandhapurise upādāya.
rows.english: The Wheel-turning Monarch travels through the air with his fourfold army, even with his grooms and shepherds.

rows.pali: Jotikassa gahapatissa puññavato iddhi.
rows.english: The householder Jotika had the success of the meritorious.

rows.pali: Jaṭilakassa gahapatissa puññavato iddhi.
rows.english: The householder Jaṭilaka had the success of the meritorious.

rows.pali: Ghositassa gahapatissa puññavato iddhi.
rows.english: The householder Ghosita had the success of the meritorious.

rows.pali: Meṇḍakassa gahapatissa puññavato iddhi.
rows.english: The householder Meṇḍaka had the success of the meritorious.

rows.pali: Pañcannaṃ mahāpuññānaṃ puññavato iddhī"ti.
rows.english: That of the five very meritorious is success of the meritorious” (Paṭis II 213).

rows.pali: Saṅkhepato pana paripākaṃ gate puññasambhāre ijjhanakaviseso puññavato iddhi.
rows.english: In brief, however, it is the distinction that consists in succeeding when the accumulated merit comes to ripen that is success of the meritorious.

rows.pali: Ettha ca jotikassa gahapatissa pathaviṃ bhinditvā maṇipāsādo uṭṭhahi.
rows.english: 41. A crystal palace cleft the earth and sprang into existence for the householder Jotika.

rows.pali: Catusaṭṭhi ca kapparukkhāti ayamassa puññavato iddhi.
rows.english: and sixty-four wishing trees. That was success of the meritorious in his case (Dhp-a IV 207).

rows.pali: Jaṭilakassa asītihattho suvaṇṇapabbato nibbatti.
rows.english: A golden rock of eighty cubits [high] was made for Jaṭilaka (Dhp-a IV 216).

rows.pali: Ghositassa sattasu ṭhānesu māraṇatthāya upakkame katepi arogabhāvo puññavato iddhi.
rows.english: Ghosita’s safe survival when attempts were made in seven places to kill him was success of the meritorious (Dhp-a I 174).

rows.pali: Meṇḍakassa ekakarīsamatte padese sattaratanamayānaṃ meṇḍakānaṃ pātubhāvo puññavato iddhi.
rows.english: The appearance to Meṇḍaka (= Ram) of rams (meṇḍaka) made of the seven gems in a place the size of one sītā 6 was success of the meritorious in Meṇḍaka (Dhp-a III 364).

rows.pali: Pañca mahāpuññā nāma meṇḍakaseṭṭhi, tassa bhariyā candapadumasirī, putto dhanañcayaseṭṭhi, suṇisā sumanadevī, dāso puṇṇo nāmāti.
rows.english: 42. The “five very meritorious” are the rich man Meṇḍaka, his wife Candapadumasiri, his son the rich man Dhanañjaya, his daughter-in-law Sumanadevī, and his slave Puṇṇa.

rows.pali: Tesu seṭṭhissa sīsaṃ nhātassa ākāsaṃ ullokanakāle aḍḍhateḷasakoṭṭhasahassāni ākāsato rattasālīnaṃ pūrenti.
rows.english: When the rich man [Meṇḍaka] washed his head and looked up at the sky, twelve thousand five hundred measures were filled for him with red rice from the sky.

rows.pali: Bhariyāya nāḷikodanamattampi gahetvā sakalajambudīpavāsike parivisamānāya bhattaṃ na khīyati.
rows.english: When his wife took a nāḷi measure of cooked rice, the food was not used up though she served the whole of Jambudīpa with it.

rows.pali: Puttassa sahassatthavikaṃ gahetvā sakalajambudīpavāsikānampi dentassa kahāpaṇā na khīyanti.
rows.english: When his son took a purse containing a thousand [ducats (kahāpaṇa)], the ducats were not exhausted even though he made gifts to all the inhabitants of Jambudīpa.

rows.pali: Suṇisāya ekaṃ vīhitumbaṃ gahetvā sakalajambudīpavāsikānampi bhājayamānāya dhaññaṃ na khīyati.
rows.english: When his daughter-in-law took a pint (tumba) measure of paddy, the grain was not used up even when she shared it out among all the inhabitants of Jambudīpa.

rows.pali: Dāsassa ekena naṅgalena kasato ito satta ito sattāti cuddasa maggā honti.
rows.english: When the slave ploughed with a single ploughshare, there were fourteen furrows, seven on each side (see Vin I 240; Dhp-a I 384).

rows.pali: Ayaṃ nesaṃ puññavato iddhi.
rows.english: This was success of the meritorious in them.

rows.pali: 378.Vijjādharādīnaṃ vehāsagamanādikā pana vijjāmayā iddhi.
rows.english: 43. (ix) That beginning with travelling through the air in the case of masters of the sciences is success through the sciences,

rows.pali: Yathāha "katamā vijjāmayā iddhi?
rows.english: according as it is said: “What is success through the sciences?

rows.pali: Vijjādharā vijjaṃ parijapitvā vehāsaṃ gacchanti.
rows.english: Masters of the sciences, having pronounced their scientific spells, travel through the air,

rows.pali: Ākāse antalikkhe hatthimpi dassenti - pe - vividhampi senābyūhaṃ dassentī"ti (paṭi. ma. 3.18).
rows.english: and they show an elephant in space, in the sky … and they show a manifold military array” (Paṭis II 213).

rows.pali: 379.Tena tena pana sammāpayogena tassa tassa kammassa ijjhanaṃ tattha tattha sammāpayogapaccayā ijjhanaṭṭhena iddhi.
rows.english: 44. (x) But the succeeding of such and such work through such and such right exertion is success in the sense of succeeding due to right exertion applied here or there,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "nekkhammena kāmacchandassa pahānaṭṭho ijjhatīti tattha tattha sammāpayogapaccayā ijjhanaṭṭhena iddhi - pe - arahattamaggena sabbakilesānaṃ pahānaṭṭho ijjhatīti tattha tattha sammāpayogapaccayā ijjhanaṭṭhena iddhī"ti (paṭi. ma. 3.18).
rows.english: according as it is said: “The meaning (purpose) of abandoning lust succeeds through renunciation, thus it is success in the sense of succeeding due to right exertion applied here or there … The meaning (purpose) of abandoning all defilements succeeds through the Arahant path, thus it is success in the sense of succeeding due to right exertion applied here or there” (Paṭis II 213).

rows.pali: Ettha ca paṭipattisaṅkhātasseva sammāpayogassa dīpanavasena purimapāḷisadisāva pāḷi āgatā.
rows.english: And the text here is similar to the previous text in the illustration of right exertion, in other words, the way.

rows.pali: Aṭṭhakathāyaṃ pana sakaṭabyūhādikaraṇavasena yaṃkiñci sippakammaṃ yaṃkiñci vejjakammaṃ tiṇṇaṃ bedānaṃ uggahaṇaṃ tiṇṇaṃ piṭakānaṃ uggahaṇaṃ antamaso kasanavapanādīni upādāya taṃ taṃ kammaṃ katvā nibbattaviseso tattha tattha sammāpayogapaccayā ijjhanaṭṭhena iddhīti āgatā. (10)
rows.english: But in the Commentary it is given as follows: “Any work belonging to a trade such as making a cart assemblage, etc., any medical work, the learning of the Three Vedas, the learning of the Three Piṭakas, even any work connected with ploughing, sowing, etc.—the distinction produced by doing such work is success in the sense of succeeding due to right exertion applied here or there.”

rows.pali: Iti imāsu dasasu iddhīsu iddhividhāyāti imasmiṃ pade adhiṭṭhānā iddhiyeva āgatā.
rows.english: 45. So, among these ten kinds of success, only (i) success by resolve is actually mentioned in the clause “kinds of supernormal power (success),”

rows.pali: Imasmiṃ panatthe vikubbanāmanomayāiddhiyopi icchitabbā eva.
rows.english: but (ii) success as transformation and (iii) success as the mind-made [body] are needed in this sense as well.

rows.pali: 380.Iddhividhāyāti iddhikoṭṭhāsāya, iddhivikappāya vā.
rows.english: 46. (i) To the kinds of supernormal power (see §20): to the components of supernormal power, or to the departments of supernormal power.

rows.pali: Cittaṃ abhinīharati abhininnāmetīti so bhikkhu vuttappakāravasena tasmiṃ citte abhiññāpādake jāte iddhividhādhigamatthāya parikammacittaṃ abhinīharati kasiṇārammaṇato apanetvā iddhividhābhimukhaṃ peseti.
rows.english: He directs, he inclines, his mind: when that bhikkhu’s consciousness has become the basis for direct-knowledge in the way already described, he directs the preliminary-work consciousness with the purpose of attaining the kinds of supernormal power, he sends it in the direction of the kinds of supernormal power, leading it away from the kasiṇa as its object.

rows.pali: Abhininnāmetīti adhigantabbaiddhipoṇaṃ iddhipabbhāraṃ karoti.
rows.english: Inclines: makes it tend and lean towards the supernormal power to be attained.

rows.pali: Soti so evaṃ katacittābhinīhāro bhikkhu.
rows.english: 47. He: the bhikkhu who has done the directing of his mind in this way.

rows.pali: Anekavihitanti anekavidhaṃ nānappakārakaṃ.
rows.english: The various: varied, of different sorts.

rows.pali: Iddhividhanti iddhikoṭṭhāsaṃ.
rows.english: Kinds of supernormal power: departments of supernormal power.

rows.pali: Paccanubhotīti paccanubhavati, phusati sacchikaroti pāpuṇātīti attho.
rows.english: Wields: paccanubhoti = paccanu-bhavati (alternative form); the meaning is that he makes contact with, realizes, reaches.

rows.pali: Idānissa anekavihitabhāvaṃ dassento "ekopi hutvā"tiādimāha.
rows.english: 48. Now, in order to show that variousness, it is said: “Having been one, [he becomes many; having been many, he becomes one. He appears and vanishes. He goes unhindered through walls, through enclosures, through mountains, as though in open space. He dives in and out of the earth as though in water. He goes on unbroken water as though on earth. Seated cross-legged he travels in space like a winged bird. With his hand he touches and strokes the moon and sun so mighty and powerful. He wields bodily mastery even as far as the Brahmā-world]” (D I 77).

rows.pali: Tattha ekopi hutvāti iddhikaraṇato pubbe pakatiyā ekopi hutvā.
rows.english: Herein, having been one: having been normally one before giving effect to the supernormal power.

rows.pali: Bahudhā hotīti bahūnaṃ santike caṅkamitukāmo vā sajjhāyaṃ vā kattukāmo pañhaṃ vā pucchitukāmo hutvā satampi sahassampi hoti.
rows.english: He becomes many: wanting to walk with many or wanting to do a recital or wanting to ask questions with many, he becomes a hundred or a thousand.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ panāyamevaṃ hoti?
rows.english: But how does he do this?

rows.pali: Iddhiyā catasso bhūmiyo cattāro pādā aṭṭha padāni soḷasa ca mūlāni sampādetvā ñāṇena adhiṭṭhahanto.
rows.english: He accomplishes, (1) the four planes, (2) the four bases (roads), (3) the eight steps, and (4) the sixteen roots of supernormal power, and then he (5) resolves with knowledge.

rows.pali: 381.Tattha catasso bhūmiyoti cattāri jhānāni veditabbāni.
rows.english: 49. 1. Herein, the four planes should be understood as the four jhānas;

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ dhammasenāpatinā "iddhiyā katamā catasso bhūmiyo?
rows.english: for this has been said by the General of the Dhamma [the Elder Sāriputta]: “What are the four planes of supernormal power?

rows.pali: Vivekajabhūmi paṭhamaṃ jhānaṃ, pītisukhabhūmi dutiyaṃ jhānaṃ, upekkhāsukhabhūmi tatiyaṃ jhānaṃ, adukkhamasukhabhūmi catutthaṃ jhānaṃ.
rows.english: They are the first jhāna as the plane born of seclusion, the second jhāna as the plane of happiness and bliss, the third jhāna as the plane of equanimity and bliss, the fourth jhāna as the plane of neither pain nor pleasure.

rows.pali: Iddhiyā imā catasso bhūmiyo iddhilābhāya iddhipaṭilābhāya iddhivikubbanatāya iddhivisavitāya iddhivasitāya iddhivesārajjāya saṃvattantī"ti (paṭi. ma. 3.9).
rows.english: These four planes of supernormal power lead to the attaining of supernormal power, to the obtaining of supernormal power, to the transformation due to supernormal power, to the majesty7 of supernormal power, to the mastery of supernormal power, to fearlessness in supernormal power” (Paṭis II 205).

rows.pali: Ettha ca purimāni tīṇi jhānāni yasmā pītipharaṇena ca sukhapharaṇena ca sukhasaññañca lahusaññañca okkamitvā lahumudukammaññakāyo iddhiṃ pāpuṇāti, tasmā iminā pariyāyena iddhilābhāya saṃvattanato sambhārabhūmiyoti veditabbāni.
rows.english: And he reaches supernormal power by becoming light, malleable and wieldy in the body after steeping himself in blissful perception and light perception due to the pervasion of happiness and pervasion of bliss, [385] which is why the first three jhānas should be understood as the accessory plane since they lead to the obtaining of supernormal power in this manner.

rows.pali: Catutthajjhānaṃ pana iddhilābhāya pakatibhūmiyeva.
rows.english: But the fourth is the natural plane for obtaining supernormal power.

rows.pali: 382.Cattāro pādāti cattāro iddhipādā veditabbā.
rows.english: 50. 2. The four bases (roads) should be understood as the four bases of success (iddhi-pāda—roads to power);

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ "iddhiyā katame cattāro pādā?
rows.english: for this is said: “What are the four bases (pāda— roads) for success (iddhi—power)?

rows.pali: Idha bhikkhu chandasamādhipadhānasaṅkhārasamannāgataṃ iddhipādaṃ bhāveti.
rows.english: Here a bhikkhu develops the basis for success (road to power) that possesses both concentration due to zeal and the will to strive (endeavour);

rows.pali: Vīriya… citta… vīmaṃsāsamādhipadhānasaṅkhārasamannāgataṃ iddhipādaṃ bhāveti.
rows.english: he develops the basis for success (road to power) that possesses both concentration due to energy and the will to strive; he develops the basis for success (road to power) that possesses both concentration due to [natural purity of] consciousness and the will to strive; he develops the basis for success (road to power) that possesses both concentration due to inquiry and the will to strive.

rows.pali: Iddhiyā ime cattāro pādā iddhilābhāya - pe - iddhivesārajjāya saṃvattantī"ti (paṭi. ma. 3.9).
rows.english: These four bases (roads) for success (power) lead to the obtaining of supernormal power (success) … to the fearlessness due to supernormal power (success)” (Paṭis II 205).

rows.pali: Ettha ca chandahetuko chandādhiko vā samādhi chandasamādhi.
rows.english: 51.And here the concentration that has zeal as its cause, or has zeal outstanding, is concentration due to zeal;

rows.pali: Kattukamyatāchandaṃ adhipatiṃ karitvā paṭiladdhasamādhissetaṃ adhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: this is a term for concentration obtained by giving precedence to zeal consisting in the desire to act.

rows.pali: Padhānabhūtā saṅkhārā padhānasaṅkhārā.
rows.english: Will (formation) as endeavour is will to strive;

rows.pali: Catukiccasādhakassa sammappadhānavīriyassetaṃ adhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: this is a term for the energy of right endeavour accomplishing its fourfold function (see §53).

rows.pali: Samannāgatanti chandasamādhinā ca padhānasaṅkhārehi ca upetaṃ.
rows.english: Possesses: is furnished with concentration due to zeal and with the [four] instances of the will to strive.

rows.pali: Iddhipādanti nipphattipariyāyena vā ijjhanaṭṭhena, ijjhanti etāya sattā iddhā vuddhā ukkaṃsagatā hontīti iminā vā pariyāyena iddhīti saṅkhaṃ gatānaṃ abhiññācittasampayuttānaṃ chandasamādhipadhānasaṅkhārānaṃ adhiṭṭhānaṭṭhena pādabhūtaṃ sesacittacetasikarāsinti attho.
rows.english: 52. Road to power (basis for success): the meaning is, the total of consciousness and its remaining concomitants [except the concentration and the will], which are, in the sense of resolve, the road to (basis for) the concentration due to zeal and will to strive associated with the direct-knowledge consciousness, which latter are themselves termed “power (success)” either by treatment as “production” (§20) or in the sense of “succeeding” (§21) or by treatment in this way, “beings succeed by its means, thus they are successful; they are enriched, promoted” (§22).

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ "iddhipādoti tathābhūtassa vedanākkhandho - pe - viññāṇakkhandho"ti (vibha. 434).
rows.english: For this is said: “Basis for success (road to power): it is the feeling aggregate, [perception aggregate, formations aggregate, and] consciousness aggregate, in one so become” (Vibh 217).

rows.pali: Atha vā pajjate anenāti pādo.
rows.english: 53. Or alternatively: it is arrived at (pajjate) by means of that, thus that is a road (pāda—basis);

rows.pali: Pāpuṇīyatīti attho.
rows.english: it is reached, is the meaning.

rows.pali: Iddhiyā pādo iddhipādo.
rows.english: Iddhi-pāda = iddhiyā pāda (resolution of compound):

rows.pali: Chandādīnametaṃ adhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: this is a term for zeal, etc.,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "chandañce, bhikkhave, bhikkhu nissāya labhati samādhiṃ, labhati cittassekaggataṃ, ayaṃ vuccati chandasamādhi.
rows.english: according as it is said: “Bhikkhus, if a bhikkhu obtains concentration, obtains unification of mind supported by zeal, this is called concentration due to zeal.

rows.pali: So anuppannānaṃ pāpakānaṃ - pe - padahati, ime vuccanti padhānasaṅkhārā.
rows.english: He [awakens zeal] for the non-arising of unarisen evil, unprofitable states, [strives, puts forth energy, strains his mind and] struggles. [He awakens zeal for the abandoning of arisen evil, unprofitable states … He awakens zeal for the arousing of unarisen profitable states … He awakens zeal for the maintenance, non-disappearance, increase, growth, development and perfection of arisen profitable states, strives, puts forth energy, strains his mind and struggles]. These are called instances of the will to strive.

rows.pali: Iti ayañca chando ayañca chandasamādhi ime ca padhānasaṅkhārā, ayaṃ vuccati, bhikkhave, chandasamādhipadhānasaṅkhārasamannāgato iddhipādo"ti (saṃ. ni. 5.825).
rows.english: So this zeal and this concentration due to zeal and these [four] instances of will to strive are called the road to power (basis for success) that possesses concentration due to zeal and the will to strive” (S V 268).

rows.pali: Evaṃ sesiddhipādesupi attho veditabbo.
rows.english: And the meaning should be understood in this way in the case of the other roads to power (bases for success).8

rows.pali: 383.Aṭṭha padānīti chandādīni aṭṭha veditabbāni.
rows.english: 54. 3. The eight steps should be understood as the eight beginning with zeal;

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ "iddhiyā katamāni aṭṭha padāni?
rows.english: for this is said: “What are the eight steps?

rows.pali: Chandañce bhikkhu nissāya labhati samādhiṃ, labhati cittassekaggataṃ.
rows.english: If a bhikkhu obtains concentration, obtains unification of mind supported by zeal,

rows.pali: Chando na samādhi, samādhi na chando.
rows.english: then the zeal is not the concentration; the concentration is not the zeal.

rows.pali: Añño chando, añño samādhi.
rows.english: The zeal is one, the concentration is another.

rows.pali: Vīriyañce bhikkhu… cittañce bhikkhu… vīmaṃsañce bhikkhu nissāya labhati samādhiṃ, labhati cittassekaggataṃ.
rows.english: If a bhikkhu … supported by energy … supported by [natural purity of] consciousness … supported by inquiry …

rows.pali: Vīmaṃsā na samādhi, samādhi na vīmaṃsā.
rows.english: then the inquiry is not the concentration; the concentration is not the inquiry.

rows.pali: Aññā vīmaṃsā, añño samādhi.
rows.english: The inquiry is one, the concentration is another.

rows.pali: Iddhiyā imāni aṭṭha padāni iddhilābhāya - pe - iddhivesārajjāya saṃvattantī"ti (paṭi. ma. 3.9).
rows.english: These eight steps to power lead to the obtaining of supernormal power (success) … to fearlessness due to supernormal power (success)” (Paṭis II 205).

rows.pali: Ettha hi iddhimuppādetukāmatāchando samādhinā ekato niyuttova iddhilābhāya saṃvattati; tathā vīriyādayo.
rows.english: For here it is the zeal consisting in the desire to arouse supernormal power (success), which zeal is joined with concentration, that leads to the obtaining of the supernormal power. Similarly in the case of energy, and so on.

rows.pali: Tasmā imāni aṭṭha padāni vuttānīti veditabbāni.
rows.english: That should be understood as the reason why they are called the “eight steps.”

rows.pali: 384.Soḷasa mūlānīti soḷasahi ākārehi āneñjatā cittassa veditabbā.
rows.english: 55. 4. The sixteen roots: the mind’s unperturbedness9 should be understood in sixteen modes,

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ – "iddhiyā kati mūlāni?
rows.english: for this is said: “What are the sixteen roots of success (power)?

rows.pali: Soḷasa mūlāni – anonataṃ cittaṃ kosajje na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, anunnataṃ cittaṃ uddhacce na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, anabhinataṃ cittaṃ rāge na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, anapanataṃ cittaṃ byāpāde na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, anissitaṃ cittaṃ diṭṭhiyā na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, appaṭibaddhaṃ cittaṃ chandarāge na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, vippamuttaṃ cittaṃ kāmarāge na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, visaṃyuttaṃ cittaṃ kilese na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, vimariyādikataṃ cittaṃ kilesamariyāde na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, ekattagataṃ cittaṃ nānattakilese na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, saddhāya pariggahitaṃ cittaṃ assaddhiye na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, vīriyena pariggahitaṃ cittaṃ kosajje na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, satiyā pariggahitaṃ cittaṃ pamāde na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, samādhinā pariggahitaṃ cittaṃ uddhacce na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, paññāya pariggahitaṃ cittaṃ avijjāya na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ, obhāsagataṃ cittaṃ avijjandhakāre na iñjatīti āneñjaṃ.
rows.english: Undejected consciousness is not perturbed by indolence, thus it is unperturbed. Unelated consciousness is not perturbed by agitation, thus it is unperturbed. Unattracted consciousness is not perturbed by greed, thus it is unperturbed. Unrepelled consciousness is not perturbed by ill will, thus it is unperturbed. Independent consciousness is not perturbed by [false] view, thus it is unperturbed. Untrammelled consciousness is not perturbed by greed accompanied by zeal, thus it is unperturbed. Liberated consciousness is not perturbed by greed for sense desires, thus it is unperturbed. Unassociated consciousness is not perturbed by defilement, thus it is unperturbed. Consciousness rid of barriers is not perturbed by the barrier of defilement, thus it is unperturbed. Unified consciousness is not perturbed by the defilement of variety, thus it is unperturbed. Consciousness reinforced by faith is not perturbed by faithlessness, thus it is unperturbed. Consciousness reinforced by energy is not perturbed by indolence, thus it is unperturbed. Consciousness reinforced by mindfulness is not perturbed by negligence, thus it is unperturbed. Consciousness reinforced by concentration is not perturbed by agitation, thus it is unperturbed. Consciousness reinforced by understanding is not perturbed by ignorance, thus it is unperturbed. Illuminated consciousness is not perturbed by the darkness of ignorance, thus it is unperturbed.

rows.pali: Iddhiyā imāni soḷasa mūlāni iddhilābhāya - pe - iddhivesārajjāya saṃvattantī"ti (paṭi. ma. 3.9).
rows.english: These sixteen roots of success (power) lead to the obtaining of supernormal power (success) … to fearlessness due to supernormal power (success)” (Paṭis II 206).

rows.pali: Kāmañca esa attho evaṃ samāhite cittetiādināpi siddhoyeva, paṭhamajjhānādīnaṃ pana iddhiyā bhūmipādapadamūlabhāvadassanatthaṃ puna vutto.
rows.english: 56. Of course, this meaning is already established by the words, “When his concentrated mind,” etc., too, but it is stated again for the purpose of showing that the first jhāna, etc., are the three planes, bases (roads), steps, and roots, of success (to supernormal powers).

rows.pali: Purimo ca suttesu āgatanayo.
rows.english: And the first-mentioned method is the one given in the Suttas,

rows.pali: Ayaṃ paṭisambhidāyaṃ.
rows.english: but this is how it is given in the Paṭisambhidā.

rows.pali: Iti ubhayattha asammohatthampi puna vutto.
rows.english: So it is stated again for the purpose of avoiding confusion in each of the two instances.

rows.pali: 385.Ñāṇena adhiṭṭhahantoti svāyamete iddhiyā bhūmipādapadabhūte dhamme sampādetvā abhiññāpādakaṃ jhānaṃ samāpajjitvā vuṭṭhāya sace sataṃ icchati "sataṃ homi sataṃ homī"ti parikammaṃ katvā puna abhiññāpādakaṃ jhānaṃ samāpajjitvā vuṭṭhāya adhiṭṭhāti, adhiṭṭhānacittena saheva sataṃ hoti.
rows.english: 57. 5. He resolves with knowledge (§48): when he has accomplished these things consisting of the planes, bases (roads), steps, and roots, of success (to supernormal power), [387] then he attains jhāna as the basis for direct-knowledge and emerges from it. Then if he wants to become a hundred, he does the preliminary work thus, “Let me become a hundred, let me become a hundred,” after which he again attains jhāna as basis for direct-knowledge, emerges, and resolves. He becomes a hundred simultaneously with the resolving consciousness.

rows.pali: Sahassādīsupi eseva nayo.
rows.english: The same method applies in the case of a thousand, and so on.

rows.pali: Sace evaṃ na ijjhati puna parikammaṃ katvā dutiyampi samāpajjitvā vuṭṭhāya adhiṭṭhātabbaṃ.
rows.english: If he does not succeed in this way, he should do the preliminary work again, and attain, emerge, and resolve a second time.

rows.pali: Saṃyuttaṭṭhakathāyaṃ hi ekavāraṃ dvevāraṃ samāpajjituṃ vaṭṭatīti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: For it is said in the Saṃyutta Commentary that it is allowable to attain once, or twice.

rows.pali: Tattha pādakajjhānacittaṃ nimittārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: 58. Herein, the basic-jhāna consciousness has the sign as its object;

rows.pali: Parikammacittāni satārammaṇāni vā sahassārammaṇāni vā, tāni ca kho vaṇṇavasena, no paṇṇattivasena.
rows.english: but the preliminary-work consciousnesses have the hundred as their object or the thousand as their object. And these latter are objects as appearances, not as concepts.

rows.pali: Adhiṭṭhānacittampi tatheva satārammaṇaṃ vā sahassārammaṇaṃ vā.
rows.english: The resolving consciousness has likewise the hundred as its object or the thousand as its object.

rows.pali: Taṃ pubbe vuttaṃ appanācittamiva gotrabhuanantaraṃ ekameva uppajjati rūpāvacaracatutthajjhānikaṃ.
rows.english: That arises once only, next to change-of-lineage [consciousness], as in the case of absorption consciousness already described (IV.78), and it is fine-material-sphere consciousness belonging to the fourth jhāna.

rows.pali: 386.Yampi paṭisambhidāyaṃ vuttaṃ "pakatiyā eko bahukaṃ āvajjati sataṃ vā sahassaṃ vā satasahassaṃ vā, āvajjitvā ñāṇena adhiṭṭhāti 'bahuko homī'ti, bahuko hoti, yathā āyasmā cūḷapanthako"ti (paṭi. ma. 3.10).
rows.english: 59. Now, it is said in the Paṭisambhidā: “Normally one, he adverts to [himself as] many or a hundred or a thousand or a hundred thousand; having adverted, he resolves with knowledge, ‘Let me be many.’ He becomes many, like the venerable Cūḷa-Panthaka” (Paṭis II 207).

rows.pali: Tatrāpi āvajjatīti parikammavaseneva vuttaṃ.
rows.english: Here he adverts is said with respect only to the preliminary work.

rows.pali: Āvajjitvā ñāṇena adhiṭṭhātīti abhiññāñāṇavasena vuttaṃ.
rows.english: Having adverted, he resolves with knowledge is said with respect to the knowledge of the direct-knowledge.

rows.pali: Tasmā bahukaṃ āvajjati, tato tesampi parikammacittānaṃ avasāne samāpajjati, samāpattito vuṭṭhahitvā puna bahuko homīti āvajjitvā tato paraṃ pavattānaṃ tiṇṇaṃ catunnaṃ vā pubbabhāgacittānaṃ anantarā uppannena sanniṭṭhāpanavasena adhiṭṭhānanti laddhanāmena ekeneva abhiññāñāṇena adhiṭṭhātīti evamettha attho daṭṭhabbo.
rows.english: Consequently, he adverts to many. After that he attains with the last one of the preliminary-work consciousnesses. After emerging from the attainment, he again adverts thus, “Let me be many,” after which he resolves by means of the single [consciousness] belonging to the knowledge of direct-knowledge, which has arisen next to the three, or four, preparatory consciousnesses that have occurred, and which has the name “resolve” owing to its making the decision. This is how the meaning should be understood here.

rows.pali: Yaṃ pana vuttaṃ "yathā āyasmā cūḷapanthako"ti, taṃ bahudhābhāvassa kāyasakkhidassanatthaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 60. Like the venerable Cūḷa-Panthaka is said in order to point to a bodily witness of this multiple state;

rows.pali: Taṃ pana vatthunā dīpetabbaṃ.
rows.english: but that must be illustrated by the story.

rows.pali: Te kira dvebhātaro panthe jātattā panthakāti nāmaṃ labhiṃsu.
rows.english: There were two brothers, it seems, who were called, “Panthaka (Roadling)” because they were born on a road.

rows.pali: Tesaṃ jeṭṭho mahāpanthako, so pabbajitvā saha paṭisambhidāhi arahattaṃ pāpuṇi.
rows.english: The senior of the two was called Mahā-Panthaka. He went forth into homelessness and reached Arahantship together with the discriminations.

rows.pali: Arahā hutvā cūḷapanthakaṃ pabbājetvā –
rows.english: When he had become an Arahant, he made Cūḷa-Panthaka go forth too,

rows.pali: Imaṃ gāthaṃ adāsi.
rows.english: - and he set him this stanza.

rows.pali: So taṃ catūhi māsehi paguṇaṃ kātuṃ nāsakkhi.
rows.english: Four months went by, but he could not get it by heart.

rows.pali: Atha naṃ thero abhabbo tvaṃ sāsaneti vihārato nīhari.
rows.english: Then the elder said, “You are useless in this dispensation,” and he expelled him from the monastery.

rows.pali: Tasmiñca kāle thero bhattuddesako hoti.
rows.english: 61. At that time the elder had charge of the allocation of meal [invitations].

rows.pali: Jīvako theraṃ upasaṅkamitvā "sve, bhante, bhagavatā saddhiṃ pañcabhikkhusatāni gahetvā amhākaṃ gehe bhikkhaṃ gaṇhathā"ti āha.
rows.english: Jīvaka approached the elder, saying, “Take alms at our house, venerable sir, together with the Blessed One and five hundred bhikkhus.”

rows.pali: Theropi ṭhapetvā cūḷapanthakaṃ sesānaṃ adhivāsemīti adhivāsesi.
rows.english: The elder consented, saying, “I accept for all but Cūḷa-Panthaka.”

rows.pali: Cūḷapanthako dvārakoṭṭhake ṭhatvā rodati.
rows.english: Cūḷa-Panthaka stood weeping at the gate.

rows.pali: Bhagavā dibbacakkhunā disvā taṃ upasaṅkamitvā kasmā rodasīti āha.
rows.english: The Blessed One saw him with the divine eye, and he went to him. “Why are you weeping?” he asked,

rows.pali: So taṃ pavattimācikkhi.
rows.english: and he was told what had happened.

rows.pali: Bhagavā na sajjhāyaṃ kātuṃ asakkonto mama sāsane abhabbo nāma hoti, mā soci bhikkhūti taṃ bāhāyaṃ gahetvā vihāraṃ pavisitvā iddhiyā pilotikakhaṇḍaṃ abhinimminitvā adāsi, handa bhikkhu imaṃ parimajjanto rajoharaṇaṃ rajoharaṇanti punappunaṃ sajjhāyaṃ karohīti.
rows.english: 62. The Blessed One said, “No one in my dispensation is called useless for being unable to do a recitation. Do not grieve, bhikkhu.” Taking him by the arm, he led him into the monastery. He created a piece of cloth by supernormal power and gave it to him, saying, “Now, bhikkhu, keep rubbing this and recite over and over again: ‘Removal of dirt, removal of dirt.’”

rows.pali: Tassa tathā karoto taṃ kāḷavaṇṇaṃ ahosi.
rows.english: While doing as he had been told, the cloth became black in colour.

rows.pali: So parisuddhaṃ vatthaṃ, natthettha doso, attabhāvassa panāyaṃ dosoti saññaṃ paṭilabhitvā pañcasu khandhesu ñāṇaṃ otāretvā vipassanaṃ vaḍḍhetvā anulomato gotrabhusamīpaṃ pāpesi.
rows.english: What he came to perceive was this: “The cloth is clean; there is nothing wrong there. It is this selfhood that is wrong.” He brought his knowledge to bear on the five aggregates, and by increasing insight he reached the neighbourhood of conformity [knowledge] and change-of-lineage [knowledge].

rows.pali: Athassa bhagavā obhāsagāthā abhāsi –
rows.english: 63.Then the Blessed One uttered these illuminative stanzas:

rows.pali: Tassa gāthāpariyosāne catupaṭisambhidāchaḷabhiññāparivārā nava lokuttaradhammā hatthagatāva ahesuṃ.
rows.english: When the stanzas were finished, the venerable Cūḷa-Panthaka had at his command the nine supramundane states attended by the four discriminations and six kinds of direct-knowledge.

rows.pali: Satthā dutiyadivase jīvakassa gehaṃ agamāsi saddhiṃ bhikkhusaṅghena.
rows.english: 64. On the following day the Master went to Jīvaka’s house together with the Community of Bhikkhus.

rows.pali: Atha dakkhiṇodakāvasāne yāguyā diyyamānāya hatthena pattaṃ pidahi.
rows.english: Then when the gruel was being given out at the end of the water-offering ceremony,11 he covered his bowl.

rows.pali: Jīvako kiṃ bhanteti pucchi.
rows.english: Jīvaka asked, “What is it, venerable sir?

rows.pali: Vihāre eko bhikkhu atthīti.
rows.english: ”—“There is a bhikkhu at the monastery.”

rows.pali: So purisaṃ pesesi "gaccha, ayyaṃ gahetvā sīghaṃ ehī"ti.
rows.english: He sent a man, telling him, “Go, and return quickly with the lord.”

rows.pali: Vihārato nikkhante pana bhagavati,
rows.english: 65. When the Blessed One had left the monastery:

rows.pali: Atha so puriso gantvā kāsāvehi ekapajjotaṃ ārāmaṃ disvā āgantvā bhikkhūhi bharito bhante ārāmo, nāhaṃ jānāmi katamo so ayyoti āha.
rows.english: 66. When the man went and saw the monastery all glowing with yellow, he returned and said, “Venerable sir, the monastery is crowded with bhikkhus. I do not know which of them the lord is.”

rows.pali: Tato naṃ bhagavā āha "gaccha yaṃ paṭhamaṃ passasi, taṃ cīvarakaṇṇe gahetvā 'satthā taṃ āmantetī'ti vatvā ānehī"ti.
rows.english: Then the Blessed One said, “Go and catch hold of the hem of the robe of the first one you see, tell him, ‘The Master calls you’ and bring him here.”

rows.pali: So taṃ gantvā therasseva cīvarakaṇṇe aggahesi.
rows.english: He went and caught hold of the elder’s robe.

rows.pali: Tāvadeva sabbepi nimmitā antaradhāyiṃsu.
rows.english: At once all the creations vanished.

rows.pali: Thero "gaccha tva"nti taṃ uyyojetvā mukhadhovanādisarīrakiccaṃ niṭṭhapetvā paṭhamataraṃ gantvā pattāsane nisīdi.
rows.english: The elder dismissed him, saying, “You may go,” and when he had finished attending to his bodily needs such as mouth washing, he arrived first and sat down on the seat prepared.

rows.pali: Idaṃ sandhāya vuttaṃ "yathā āyasmā cūḷapanthako"ti.
rows.english: It was with reference to this that it was said, “like the venerable Cūḷa-Panthaka.”

rows.pali: Tatra ye te bahū nimmitā te aniyametvā nimmitattā iddhimatā sadisāva honti.
